title: The Gaelic Athlete, vol. VIII, no. 185, Saturday July 17th 1915

pages.text:
July 17, 1815

Ireland's Record in Athletic Sports

From the Tailtean Games to the Present Time

Including the Complete History of the Rise and Development of the G.A.A.

By "VIGILANT."

XXII. - The G.A.A. in 1886 (continued).

The second annual athletic championship meeting held by the Gaelic Athletic Association took place at Ballinlough, Dublin, on September 11th, 1886, and produced some remarkably good performances. Mitchell made new records with the 60 lb. weight and the 16 lb. hammer.

The second annual convention which was to be held on the anniversary of the founding of the Association, November 1st, 1886, was not held until a fortnight after that date had elapsed. There was a big attendance of delegates present. Mr. Maurice Davin presided, and some very useful legislation for the better development of the games was accomplished. Wrestling in football matches was abolished. The formation of county committees was approved. The constitution of the Association was in many respects revised and many emendations were made to the hurling and football rules. The officials were elected as follows: President, Mr. Maurice Davin; Vice-Presidents, Messrs. J. K. Bracken, Templemore; J. E. Kennedy, Dublin; P. F. Moloney, Nenagh; P. T. Hackett, Hon. Sec., J. R. O'Reilly, Dublin; J. Wyse-Power, Dublin; and T. O'Riordan, Cork; Recording Secretary, Mr. James Butler, Thurles. Mr. John Clancy, Dublin, resigned the position of hand-piper to the Association, and Mr. F. H. Dinneen, County Limerick, was elected to the position. Mr. J. F. Crowley, Cork, resigned the position of hand-piper to the Association, and Mr. F. H. Dinneen, County Limerick, was elected to the position.

Mr. John O'Leary wrote the following characteristic letter to the convention accepting the position of patron to the Association:

"Dublin, November 17th, 1886.

" Gentlemen, I need not say that I am grateful to the Gaelic Athletic Association for the honour they have done me, an honour the more highly prized in that I am the first Irishman to hold such an office, and objects of the Association. There are many societies and associations in Ireland, and I am glad to see that the G.A.A. is not only the first Irish association to hold such an office, but that it is the only one to do so.
